name: Beratón Pass via Cara Oculta del Moncayo
name_original: Subida al Puerto de Beratón desde Trasobares — Cara Oculta del Moncayo
description: This moderate 31.6-mile road cycling route climbs to the Port of Beratón from Trasobares, offering challenging ascents and Moncayo views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Trasobares are set within the picturesque Isuela river valley in the province of Zaragoza, Aragón. The region features winding roads and varied terrain, offering engaging environments for cyclists. The landscape includes charming, shallow, grassy valleys dotted with olive trees and views of the Rio Isuela crags. Longer rides can encompass the diverse natural monuments and protected landscapes characteristic of the broader Zaragoza province. Best road cycling routes around Trasobares The most popular road cycling route is Aranda Comarca Loop, a 50.5 miles (81.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 53 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Templars' Path Loop, a moderate 24.6 miles (39.6 km) path. This route offers scenic views of the Isuela river valley and surrounding olive groves. Local road cyclists also love the Beratón Pass via Cara Oculta del Moncayo, a 31.6 miles (50.8 km) trail leading through mountainous landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 37 minutes. Road cycling around Trasobares is defined by winding roads, river valley scenery, and varied elevation. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to challenging climbs. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Trasobares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Trasobares?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;over 5 road cycling routes&lt;/strong&gt; around Trasobares, catering to various skill levels. These routes explore the picturesque Isuela river valley and the surrounding varied terrain.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Trasobares?
A: While many routes offer a challenge, you can find moderate options. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992909482/templars-path-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Templars' Path Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 24.6-mile (39.6 km) path that offers scenic views of the Isuela river valley and olive groves, making it a good choice for those looking for a less strenuous ride.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Trasobares?
A: Road cycling around Trasobares is characterized by &lt;strong&gt;winding roads and varied elevation&lt;/strong&gt;. The region is nestled in the Isuela river valley, offering riverine landscapes, views of the Rio Isuela crags, and charming grassy valleys dotted with olive trees. Longer rides can extend into the broader Zaragoza province, encompassing diverse natural monuments and protected landscapes.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Trasobares offers several challenging routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989952691/aranda-comarca-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Comarca Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 50.5-mile (81.2 km) trail with significant elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151111" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bar de Moncayo – Castillo de Vozmediano loop from Tierga&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 94 miles (151 km) with substantial climbing.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or natural attractions I can see while cycling?
A: The region offers numerous scenic spots. You might encounter views of the Rio Isuela crags and olive groves along routes. For specific natural attractions, consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288045" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de los Pilares&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Morrón Summit&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5271458" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valcongosto Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. The broader Aragón region is rich in natural parks and protected landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Trasobares?
A: Given the region's climate, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures. Summers can be hot, especially in the valley, while winters might bring colder weather, though the area is not known for extreme snowfall at lower elevations.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Trasobares?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989952691/aranda-comarca-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Comarca Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992909482/templars-path-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Templars' Path Loop&lt;/a&gt;, both offering a complete circuit.

Q: What facilities are available in Trasobares for cyclists, such as cafes or accommodation?
A: Trasobares is a small village, but the surrounding area, including nearby towns, offers amenities. While specific cafes or hotels directly on routes are not detailed, you can typically find places to eat and stay in the larger settlements within the Zaragoza province. It's advisable to plan your stops in advance, especially on longer rides.

Q: Is there parking available for road cyclists in Trasobares?
A: While specific designated cycling parking areas are not highlighted, Trasobares is a village with accessible roads. You can usually find parking within or on the outskirts of the village, allowing you to start your cycling adventures conveniently. Always check local regulations for parking.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Trasobares?
A: Road cyclists often praise the region for its &lt;strong&gt;varied terrain and scenic beauty&lt;/strong&gt;. The winding roads, combined with views of the Isuela river valley and olive groves, provide an engaging and picturesque cycling experience. The opportunity to explore diverse natural landscapes within the broader Zaragoza province is also a significant draw.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes starting from Trasobares?
A: Yes, for those seeking longer rides, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151111" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bar de Moncayo – Castillo de Vozmediano loop from Tierga&lt;/a&gt; offer a substantial distance of over 94 miles (151 km), allowing for extended exploration of the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through the Comarca del Aranda region?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989952691/aranda-comarca-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Comarca Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a prominent route that takes you through this area, offering a challenging ride with significant elevation gain and showcasing the characteristics of the Comarca del Aranda.
tips: From this point, on the outskirts of Calcena, you have a good view of the whole town with its traditional houses and its magnificent natural environment.

Located on the Hidden Face of Moncayo, Beratón offers us wonderful views as soon as we crown its port. The climb from Calcena (where the climb begins) is not very demanding but it is very entertaining.

Located in the Aranda Region and on the Hidden Face of Moncayo, we find this beautiful town of Purujosa where you can breathe tranquility together with the wonderful views of the nature that surrounds it, such as the Moncayo Natural Park. It is recommended to visit the Mirador, the hermitage and its streets.

Viewpoint located in a privileged environment such as the town of Purujosa, much of its term within the Moncayo Natural Park. From this viewpoint we will have magnificent views of the Barranco del Cuartel or Virgen ravine and the Sierra del Moncayo.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the climb to the Port of Beratón from Trasobares?
faqs.answer: While listed for racebikes, the route traverses the 'Hidden Face of Moncayo' region, which is primarily known for mountain biking. You should be prepared for potentially variable road conditions, including rough asphalt or even unpaved sections, especially as you approach Beratón. The terrain is rugged and mountainous, offering a significant physical challenge.

faqs.question: How difficult is this climb for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: The climb is rated as moderate, but given the nature of the 'Hidden Face of Moncayo' region, it can be quite challenging for typical road bikes. Expect steep gradients and potentially rough surfaces that might be more suited to gravel or mountain bikes. It's a demanding route that requires good physical condition.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Moncayo region offers beautiful scenery, but its mountainous nature means weather can vary. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring snow or icy conditions, making the climb more challenging or impassable.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers several scenic spots. You can enjoy a View of Calcena, pass through the charming village of Beratón (the highest village in Soria province), and also visit Purujosa. There's also a stunning View of Barranco del Cuartel and Sierra del Moncayo from Purujosa, offering panoramic vistas of the Moncayo massif.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route in Trasobares?
faqs.answer: Trasobares is a small village. While specific designated cycling parking isn't widely documented, you should be able to find suitable street parking within or on the outskirts of the village. Always ensure you park responsibly and do not obstruct local traffic or residents' access.

faqs.question: Does the route pass through any natural parks or protected areas, and are there any permits or fees required?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is located within the Parque Natural del Moncayo. Generally, there are no entrance fees for the park itself. However, it's always advisable to check local regulations regarding cycling in specific areas, especially if you plan to deviate from established roads. No specific cycling permits are typically required for public roads.

faqs.question: Is the Climb to the Port of Beratón from Trasobares dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route, dogs are generally allowed if they are safely secured (e.g., in a bike trailer) and do not interfere with traffic or other users. If you plan to stop and explore any natural areas off the road, always keep your dog on a leash, especially within the Parque Natural del Moncayo, to protect wildlife and ensure their safety.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the rugged and beautiful landscapes of the Moncayo Natural Park. Expect a mix of rocky areas, dense holm oak forests, and even beech forests on the northern slopes. The route offers impressive views of the Moncayo massif, with peaks reaching over 2,000 meters, particularly around Beratón and Purujosa.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several other trails. You'll find it intersects with parts of the [CIMA ZA02] Beratón, Ruta 1 Cerro Gordo, Etapa 3: Purujosa - Calcena, and Etapa 4: Calcena - Tierga, among others. This offers opportunities to explore different paths if you're looking for variations.

faqs.question: Are there any places to eat or stay near the start or end of the route?
faqs.answer: Trasobares and Beratón are small villages, so options might be limited. It's recommended to check in advance for local cafeterias, restaurants, or accommodation. Larger towns in the vicinity might offer more choices, but planning ahead is key for this more remote region.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this climb?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging terrain and potential for variable conditions, it's crucial to be well-prepared. Bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike, and appropriate clothing layers. A helmet is essential. Consider a GPS device or a fully charged phone with offline maps, as cell service can be spotty in remote areas.

waypoints.name: View of Barranco del Cuartel and Sierra del Moncayo from Purujosa
